description: Architecture model for the Oracle Project Management system, supporting developers and managers through a web portal and Telegram bot.

decisions.content:
# ADR-001: Layered and Client-Server Architecture

**Date:** 2025-05-01
**Status:** Accepted
**Consulted:** Full development team, Project Manager

---

## 1. Context

The Oracle Project Management system must support two distinct user interfaces —
a web portal and a Telegram bot — both requiring access to the same business
logic and data. Without a defined structural organization, business logic would
be duplicated across interfaces, data access would be inconsistent, and the
system would become difficult to extend or maintain.

The team needed a structure that enforced clear separation between presentation,
business rules, and data persistence while satisfying reliability and
maintainability requirements. The existing tech stack is Node.js with an Oracle
ATP database deployed on OCI.

---

## 2. Decision

We will organize the system using a **Layered (N-tier) Architecture** combined
with a **Client-Server** style as the foundational structural approach.

The layers are defined as follows:

- **Presentation Layer**: Web Portal UI and Telegram Bot UI. Responsible solely
  for user interaction. Contains no business logic.
- **Integration Layer**: REST API Backend (`server.js`) and Bot Integration
  Adapter (`bot.js`). Single entry point for all client requests.
- **Application Layer**: Auth & Access, Task Management, Team Activity, KPI &
  Reporting, and Gemini Service. Contains all business rules and domain logic.
- **Data Layer**: Oracle ATP Database. Responsible for persistent storage.

The Client-Server style is applied by treating the Web Portal and Telegram Bot
as independent clients that communicate exclusively with the REST API Backend.
Neither client accesses the database or application services directly.

---

## 3. Alternatives Considered

| Tension Point | Layered + Client-Server | Alternative |
|---|---|---|
| Code duplication across interfaces | Single backend serves both clients via REST | Each interface implements its own logic — rejected due to duplication and inconsistency |
| Separation of concerns | Each layer has one responsibility | Monolithic single-file approach — rejected due to poor maintainability |
| Extensibility for new clients | New clients consume the same REST API | Tight coupling per interface — rejected because adding a mobile app would require rewriting logic |

---

## 4. Consequences

**Positive (The Gains):**
- Both interfaces share the same business rules, ensuring consistent behavior across channels.
- Each layer can be modified independently without affecting the others.
- New clients (mobile app, third-party integrations) can be added without changes to the backend.
- Each layer can be tested in isolation.

**Negative (The Pains):**
- All requests must pass through the REST API layer, adding a network hop compared to direct access.
- The REST API Backend is a single point of failure; its unavailability affects all clients simultaneously.
decisions.title: ADR-001: Layered and Client-Server Architecture

decisions.content:
# ADR-002: Component-Based Design with Single Responsibility

**Date:** 2025-05-01
**Status:** Accepted
**Consulted:** Full development team, Project Manager

---

## 1. Context

The system handles several distinct business concerns: task management, KPI
calculation, team activity tracking, authentication, and AI integration. The
team also needed to integrate Google Gemini as an external AI service without
coupling it to the core task logic.

If these responsibilities were bundled into a single module, changes to one
concern would risk breaking others. The codebase would become difficult to
test, maintain, or extend — especially given that the AI provider may change
in the future and the team is working under sprint-based delivery constraints.

---

## 2. Decision

We will structure the backend using a **Component-Based Architecture** style,
assigning each functional concern to a dedicated component with a single,
well-defined responsibility (Single Responsibility Principle at the
architectural level).

The components and their responsibilities are:

| Component | File | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Bot Integration Adapter | `bot.js` | Receives Telegram commands, translates them into REST calls |
| REST API Backend | `server.js` | Exposes HTTP endpoints, coordinates between clients and services |
| Auth & Access Service | `server.js` (auth module) | Validates identity and manages permissions |
| Task Management Service | `server.js` (task module) | Handles task retrieval, creation, and status updates |
| Team Activity Service | `server.js` (activity module) | Tracks and exposes team actions and task changes |
| KPI & Reporting Service | `server.js` (kpi module) | Calculates and exposes productivity metrics |
| Gemini Service | `geminiService.js` | Manages all communication with Google Gemini API |

Components communicate through defined interfaces (HTTP/REST or internal calls),
never through shared mutable state.

---

## 3. Alternatives Considered

| Tension Point | Component-Based | Alternative |
|---|---|---|
| AI provider coupling | Gemini logic isolated in `geminiService.js`, replaceable independently | Inline Gemini calls inside `server.js` — rejected because changing AI provider would require modifying the entire backend |
| Business logic organization | Each concern in a dedicated module | Single `server.js` handling everything — rejected due to high coupling and poor testability |
| Team parallel development | Components owned independently by team members | Shared monolithic file — rejected due to merge conflicts and unclear ownership |

---

## 4. Consequences

**Positive (The Gains):**
- Each component can be modified or replaced independently as long as its interface remains stable.
- The Gemini Service can be swapped for a different AI provider by changing only `geminiService.js`.
- Team members can own and develop components in parallel with minimal conflicts.
- Components can be unit tested in isolation by mocking their dependencies.

**Negative (The Pains):**
- Multiple components must collaborate to fulfill a single user request, requiring clear interface contracts.
- Defining component boundaries upfront requires more design effort than a simple monolithic approach.
decisions.title: ADR-002: Component-Based Design with Single Responsibility

decisions.content:
# ADR-003: Event-Driven Architecture for AI Feature

**Date:** 2025-05-01
**Status:** Accepted
**Consulted:** Full development team, Project Manager

---

## 1. Context

The system includes an AI-powered feature that monitors developer activity and
automatically generates reports for the Project Manager when tasks have not
been updated within a sprint or hours have not been logged. This feature cannot
rely on a direct user action — it must fire automatically based on conditions
detected in the system.

A synchronous or request-driven approach would require the manager to manually
trigger the check, which eliminates the value of automation. The team needed a
mechanism that reacts to conditions as they emerge, not on demand. The
constraint is that the system runs on OCI with a Node.js backend and must
integrate with Google Gemini API for the AI processing.

---

## 2. Decision

We will implement the AI monitoring feature using an **Event-Driven
Architecture** style. The flow is triggered by two types of events:

1. **Scheduled event**: A scheduler runs periodically and emits a trigger when
   the inactivity check interval is reached, initiating the monitoring cycle
   automatically without user interaction.
2. **Command event**: A developer sends a Telegram command (e.g. `/update`,
   `/status`). The bot receives it as an event and routes it to the appropriate
   service.

The event-driven flow for the AI feature:

```
Scheduler trigger
  → Gemini Service queries Task Management Service for stale tasks
  → Gemini Service sends context prompt to Google Gemini API
  → Gemini API returns analysis and report content
  → Gemini Service persists report to Oracle ATP Database
  → Telegram Bot delivers report notification to Project Manager
```

---

## 3. Alternatives Considered

| Tension Point | Event-Driven | Alternative |
|---|---|---|
| Report generation trigger | Scheduler fires automatically based on inactivity conditions | Manager manually requests report via portal — rejected because it requires human intervention and defeats the automation purpose |
| Decoupling of monitoring logic | Scheduler only emits trigger; Gemini Service processes independently | Scheduler directly calls Gemini API — rejected because it creates tight coupling between scheduling and AI logic |
| Handling external AI failures | Gemini Service handles retries and error logging independently | Inline error handling in scheduler — rejected because it mixes scheduling concerns with AI error management |

---

## 4. Consequences

**Positive (The Gains):**
- The Project Manager receives reports automatically without manual requests, improving visibility into team activity.
- The scheduler and Gemini Service are decoupled; the scheduler has no knowledge of what processes its trigger.
- New event types (sprint end, milestone reached) can be added as triggers without modifying existing components.
- The system reacts to real conditions (task inactivity) rather than waiting for explicit user requests.

**Negative (The Pains):**
- Automated flows are harder to trace and debug than synchronous interactions; explicit logging and monitoring are required.
- The feature depends on Google Gemini API availability; if the external service is down, report generation fails unless retry logic is implemented.
- The relevance of AI reports depends on the scheduler interval being correctly configured for the team's sprint cadence.
decisions.title: ADR-003: Event-Driven Architecture for AI Feature

**Date:** 2025-05-01
**Status:** Accepted
**Consulted:** Full development team, Project Manager

Neither client accesses the database or application services directly.

|---|---|---|
| Code duplication across interfaces | Single backend serves both clients via REST | Each interface implements its own logic — rejected due to duplication and inconsistency |

**Negative (The Pains):**

- The REST API Backend is a single point of failure; its unavailability affects all clients simultaneously.

# ADR-002: Component-Based Design with Single Responsibility

**Date:** 2025-05-01
**Status:** Accepted
**Consulted:** Full development team, Project Manager

---

## 1. Context

| Tension Point | Component-Based | Alternative |
|---|---|---|
| AI provider coupling | Gemini logic isolated in `geminiService.js`, replaceable independently | Inline Gemini calls inside `server.js` — rejected because changing AI provider would require modifying the entire backend |
| Business logic organization | Each concern in a dedicated module | Single `server.js` handling everything — rejected due to high coupling and poor testability |
| Team parallel development | Components owned independently by team members | Shared monolithic file — rejected due to merge conflicts and unclear ownership |

**Positive (The Gains):**
- Each component can be modified or replaced independently as long as its interface remains stable.

- Components can be unit tested in isolation by mocking their dependencies.

**Negative (The Pains):**
- Multiple components must collaborate to fulfill a single user request, requiring clear interface contracts.
- Defining component boundaries upfront requires more design effort than a simple monolithic approach.

# ADR-003: Event-Driven Architecture for AI Feature

**Date:** 2025-05-01
**Status:** Accepted
**Consulted:** Full development team, Project Manager

---

## 1. Context

The system includes an AI-powered feature that monitors developer activity and
automatically generates reports for the Project Manager when tasks have not

rely on a direct user action — it must fire automatically based on conditions
detected in the system.

A synchronous or request-driven approach would require the manager to manually
trigger the check, which eliminates the value of automation. The team needed a

```
Scheduler trigger
  → Gemini Service queries Task Management Service for stale tasks
  → Gemini Service sends context prompt to Google Gemini API
  → Gemini API returns analysis and report content
  → Gemini Service persists report to Oracle ATP Database
  → Telegram Bot delivers report notification to Project Manager
```

---

| Tension Point | Event-Driven | Alternative |
|---|---|---|
| Report generation trigger | Scheduler fires automatically based on inactivity conditions | Manager manually requests report via portal — rejected because it requires human intervention and defeats the automation purpose |
| Decoupling of monitoring logic | Scheduler only emits trigger; Gemini Service processes independently | Scheduler directly calls Gemini API — rejected because it creates tight coupling between scheduling and AI logic |
| Handling external AI failures | Gemini Service handles retries and error logging independently | Inline error handling in scheduler — rejected because it mixes scheduling concerns with AI error management |

---

- The Project Manager receives reports automatically without manual requests, improving visibility into team activity.
- The scheduler and Gemini Service are decoupled; the scheduler has no knowledge of what processes its trigger.
- New event types (sprint end, milestone reached) can be added as triggers without modifying existing components.
- The system reacts to real conditions (task inactivity) rather than waiting for explicit user requests.

**Negative (The Pains):**
- Automated flows are harder to trace and debug than synchronous interactions; explicit logging and monitoring are required.
- The feature depends on Google Gemini API availability; if the external service is down, report generation fails unless retry logic is implemented.
- The relevance of AI reports depends on the scheduler interval being correctly configured for the team's sprint cadence.

Sends commands to check and update tasks

people.description: Supervises team tasks, reviews KPI metrics, monitors team activity, and receives AI-generated reports.

relationships.description: Reviews KPIs, monitors activity, receives AI reports
Uses to review KPIs and monitor team

softwareSystems.description: External messaging platform. Delivers messages between users and the bot using the token issued by BotFather.

softwareSystems.description: External AI service used to detect inactive developers, understand blockers, and generate automated reports for the project manager.
softwareSystems.name: Google Gemini API

containers.description: Browser-based interface for developers and managers to manage tasks, view KPIs, and monitor team activity.
containers.name: Web Portal

components.description: Receives Telegram updates, parses commands, translates them into REST calls to the backend API, and formats responses back to Telegram.
components.name: Bot Integration Adapter

relationships.description: Requests task data and status updates

containers.description: Conversational interface on Telegram. Receives commands, forwards requests to the backend, and returns formatted responses to the user.
containers.name: Telegram Bot (bot.js)

components.description: Validates user identity, manages permissions, and ensures secure access to all system functions.
components.name: Auth & Access Service

components.description: Handles task retrieval, status updates, assignment queries, and project data consultation.

relationships.description: Provides task data for KPI calculation

components.description: Tracks and exposes recent team actions, task changes, and collaboration activity.
components.name: Team Activity Service

relationships.description: Feeds activity data into productivity metrics

components.description: Calculates and exposes productivity indicators, sprint progress metrics, and team-level KPI dashboards.
components.name: KPI & Reporting Service

relationships.description: Reads KPI records and writes computed metrics

components.description: Detects developers with inactive tasks, sends contextual prompts to Google Gemini API, parses the AI response, and triggers automated report generation for the project manager.
components.name: Gemini Service (GeminiService.js)

relationships.description: Sends prompt with task context, receives AI analysis
Sends contextual prompts and receives analysis
Queries tasks with no recent updates

containers.description: Central entry point for all business operations. Exposes REST endpoints consumed by the web portal and the Telegram bot.
containers.name: REST API Backend (Server.js)

containers.description: Persistent storage for tasks, users, KPI records, activity logs, and AI-generated reports.
containers.name: Oracle ATP Database

softwareSystems.description: Centralised system that allows developers and managers to manage tasks, track KPIs, monitor team activity, and automate follow-up via AI.
softwareSystems.name: Oracle Project Management System
